title: Box Packing

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: You have a 6 x 6 x 6 box and 53 bricks of dimension 1 x 1 x 4 each.
rules: Each brick has volume 4 cubic units.
Bricks are rigid and can be oriented along any of the three axes.
Bricks must lie aligned with the integer grid -- no tilting or fractional positions.
The box has volume 6^3 = 216 cubic units. The total brick volume is 53 * 4 = 212 cubic units.
setup.question: Can the 53 bricks be packed into the box?

learningObjectives: Recognize that volume-fits doesn't imply packing-fits.
Construct a 3-coloring or 2-coloring that any valid placement must respect.
Convert a coloring imbalance into a hard upper bound on placeable pieces.

stages.label: Comprehension
stages.prompt: Total brick volume vs. box volume: do the numbers FIT?

options.text: Yes -- 53 * 4 = 212 < 216 = 6^3, so volume alone says it fits.

stages.explanation: Volume fits with 4 units to spare. Yet the answer turns out to be NO -- you can't pack them. That gap (4 units of slack but still impossible) is what makes this a coloring problem.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: Volume fits but you suspect packing fails. What's the right tool?

options.text: A coloring argument: assign each cell a color, count what each brick must cover, find a mismatch.

stages.explanation: Coloring arguments are the standard tool for proving impossibility when volume fits. Pick a coloring that the brick MUST disrupt unevenly, and the impossibility falls out from counting colors.

stages.label: Pick the right coloring
stages.prompt: Divide the 6 x 6 x 6 box into 27 small 2 x 2 x 2 sub-cubes (3 x 3 x 3 of them), and color them like a 3D checkerboard. How many sub-cubes are each color?

options.text: 13 of one color, 14 of the other (because 27 is odd).

stages.explanation: 27 = 3^3 sub-cubes, alternating: in a 3x3x3 cube the corners are one color and the centers/midpoints alternate. Result: 14 of one color, 13 of the other. The asymmetry between 14 and 13 will be the load-bearing fact.

stages.label: Brick coverage
stages.prompt: Place a 1 x 1 x 4 brick anywhere in the box (any axis, any position). How many sub-cube colors does it touch?

options.text: Always one color (it stays in one sub-cube).
Exactly one black sub-cube + exactly one white sub-cube. (It spans two adjacent sub-cubes, one of each color.)

stages.explanation: A 1x1x4 brick is exactly the length of two sub-cubes laid end-to-end (each sub-cube is 2 units long). So the brick spans exactly 2 adjacent sub-cubes -- which must be different colors in the checkerboard. So every brick covers 1 black sub-cube and 1 white sub-cube.

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.prompt: Each sub-cube has volume 2^3 = 8 and can hold at most 8/4 = 2... wait, more carefully: each sub-cube can hold up to 4 bricks (each brick uses 1/2 of the sub-cube's volume in the brick's axis). With 13 sub-cubes of the minority color, each holding at most 4 brick-halves, what's the maximum number of bricks placeable?

options.text: 52, since each brick consumes one minority-color sub-cube slot, and only 13 * 4 = 52 are available.
26, since each brick uses two sub-cubes.

stages.explanation: Each brick consumes exactly 1/4 of a minority-color sub-cube's volume (since the brick uses 1 column of length 4 inside the 2x2x2 = 8-volume sub-cube, that's 4/8 = 1/2 ... actually each brick uses one 1x1 column at the boundary between two sub-cubes -- 4 such columns fit per minority sub-cube). So at most 13 * 4 = 52 bricks fit. The 53rd has nowhere to go. NOT possible.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Could 52 bricks be packed? (The argument doesn't say they can; coloring is one-way. What might you try to prove possibility?)
If the box were 8 x 8 x 8 (volume 512) and you had 127 bricks of size 1x1x4 (volume 508), would the same argument apply?
Why does this argument REQUIRE 2x2x2 sub-cubes? Why not 1x1x1 single-cell coloring?
